figures.caption: Figure 2: Overview of the dual-track evaluation framework. The semantic track uses an LVM judge to score instruction following (IF), text accuracy (TA), visual consistency (VC), layout preservation (LP), and language/script fidelity (LSF) from the source image, model output, reference image, and editing instruction. The pixel track evaluates background preservation on the non-edited region defined by the union and dilation of source and target text masks.

Figure 3: Two-stage protocol for language/script fidelity (LSF) scoring. The judge first traces the edited target text by comparing the source image, reference image, and model output, then scores script-level fidelity using only the expected and predicted target text, focusing on character correctness, diacritics or tonal marks, writing directionality, and script purity.
Figure 1: Overview of the MULTITEXTEDIT data construction pipeline.

Table 4: Language coverage and cross-lingual degradation relative to English, aggregated across 12 model settings. Script, Dir., and Res. indicate writing system, text directionality, and resource level (H = High, MH = Mid-High, M = Mid, L = Low). SEMAVG is the average semantic score, and Std denotes the standard deviation of model-wise ∆Sem values.
Table 3: Overall results on MULTITEXTEDIT. SEMAVG is the mean of IF, TA, LSF, VC, and LP. EN and Non-EN denote semantic averages on the English and non-English subsets; Gap is their difference.

Figure 4: Controlled cross-lingual comparison under the same edit template. Only the target text varies across languages, while semantic errors become more pronounced from Spanish to Arabic despite largely preserved layout.
